past_medical_history: Type 2 diabetes mellitus, diagnosed 12 years ago, controlled on oral agents
Benign prostatic hyperplasia, diagnosed 5 years ago
family_history: Mother had Alzheimer disease, diagnosed at age 75, passed at 82
Father had hypertension and type 2 diabetes, died of stroke at 71
One brother, age 70, type 2 diabetes and early cognitive complaints
Maternal aunt had dementia, diagnosed in her late 70s

social_history.education: Associate's degree in business, attended community college
social_history.living_situation: Lives with wife of 50 years in a two-story home in suburban neighborhood
social_history.tobacco: Former smoker, 15 pack-years, quit 20 years ago
social_history.exercise: Previously walked the neighborhood daily, has decreased activity over the past 8 months

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person and place; knows the month and year but provides the wrong date by four days. MMSE 21/30, losing points on recall (0/3 at 5 minutes), orientation (2 points), attention/serial sevens (3 errors), and delayed copy of intersecting pentagons. Clock drawing reveals mildly distorted spacing of numbers with minute and hour hands reversed for 'ten past eleven.' Cannot recall any of 3 registered words at 5 minutes, even with category and multiple-choice cues. Naming intact for common objects but cannot name the band on a wristwatch or the eraser on a pencil. Repetition intact. Follows two-step commands but fails three-step commands.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal, round, and reactive. Bilateral pseudophakic lenses noted. Extraocular movements full, no nystagmus. Visual fields full to confrontation. Face symmetric with normal strength. Hearing mildly diminished bilaterally, age-appropriate. Palate elevates symmetrically. Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius 5/5 bilaterally. Tongue midline without atrophy or fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Normal bulk and tone throughout. No rigidity or cogwheeling. Strength 5/5 in all extremities proximally and distally. No pronator drift. No tremor or adventitious movements.
neurological_exam.sensory: Intact to light touch and pinprick in all extremities. Vibration sense mildly reduced at bilateral great toes, consistent with age. Proprioception intact at toes bilaterally.
neurological_exam.reflexes: 2+ and symmetric at biceps, triceps, brachioradialis, and patellar tendons bilaterally. Ankle jerks 1+ bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose and heel-to-shin intact bilaterally without dysmetria. Rapid alternating movements normal.
neurological_exam.gait: Steady gait with normal base and stride. Tandem gait intact. Romberg negative. No festination or shuffling.
neurological_exam.additional: No paratonic rigidity. No grasp reflexes. No myoclonus. No snout or palmomental reflexes.

patient.chief_complaint: Progressive memory loss over approximately two years, brought in by wife who is concerned about worsening forgetfulness
patient.history_present_illness: Mr. Takeshi Yamamoto is a 81-year-old right-handed retired postal carrier who presents to the neurology outpatient clinic accompanied by his wife, Doris, for evaluation of progressive memory difficulties over the past two years. His wife reports that she first noticed changes roughly two years ago when he began misplacing his keys and wallet with increasing frequency, which she initially attributed to normal aging. However, over the past twelve months the problems have become substantially worse and harder to dismiss. He now frequently forgets conversations they had earlier the same day and asks the same questions repeatedly, sometimes three or four times in a single afternoon. He has been an avid fisherman for decades but last summer left his tackle box at the lake on two separate occasions and once drove to a different boat launch than the one he had used for thirty years, becoming confused about which road to take. His wife reports that she discovered unpaid utility bills stuffed in a desk drawer three months ago; he had always handled the household finances meticulously and this was deeply out of character. She has since taken over bill paying and checkbook management. He missed two follow-up appointments with his primary care physician despite having them written on the kitchen calendar, apparently forgetting to check it. At their church, where he has been a deacon for twenty years, fellow members have remarked that he seems quieter than usual and occasionally loses his train of thought during conversations. His wife also notes that he tried to assemble a new bookshelf from a kit two months ago, a task that would have been straightforward for him previously, but became frustrated and confused by the instructions and eventually gave up. He continues to dress, bathe, and feed himself independently, though his wife has begun laying out his clothes because he sometimes picks out mismatched outfits. He still drives locally on familiar routes but his wife no longer feels comfortable with him driving at night or in unfamiliar areas. He denies any hallucinations, personality changes, or aggressive behavior. He has no history of falls, seizures, focal weakness, or tremor. His sleep pattern is unchanged and his wife denies witnessing any acting out of dreams. His appetite is fair, and he has lost approximately five pounds over the past six months without trying. He appears somewhat subdued during the interview and occasionally looks to his wife for confirmation of details but does not appear overtly depressed.

signal_characteristics.T1: Bilateral hippocampal volume loss with prominent choroidal fissures and widened temporal horns
signal_characteristics.T2_FLAIR: No abnormal signal within hippocampal formations

findings.location: Medial temporal lobes bilaterally, right slightly greater than left

signal_characteristics.Coronal_T1: Enlarged temporal horns of lateral ventricles with widened perihippocampal CSF spaces bilaterally

signal_characteristics.T1: Widened cortical sulci in bilateral temporal and parietal convexities, disproportionate to expected age-related changes

signal_characteristics.T2_FLAIR: Punctate and early confluent periventricular hyperintensities with scattered deep white matter foci, consistent with chronic small vessel ischemic disease in the setting of vascular risk factors

volumetrics.hippocampal_volume: Bilateral hippocampal atrophy, Scheltens MTA score 3 on right, 2-3 on left
volumetrics.ventricular_system: Moderate lateral ventriculomegaly, predominantly ex vacuo, with disproportionate temporal horn enlargement
volumetrics.cortical_thickness: Reduced cortical thickness in bilateral temporal and parietal association cortices

additional_observations: No acute infarcts on DWI sequences
No mass lesions or space-occupying processes
No evidence of hemorrhage or microbleeds on SWI
Patent intracranial vasculature on MRA without evidence of significant stenosis
No abnormal leptomeningeal or parenchymal enhancement
mri.impression: Bilateral hippocampal and medial temporal lobe atrophy (Scheltens MTA grade 2-3 left, grade 3 right) with temporoparietal-predominant cortical volume loss disproportionate to age. Mild-to-moderate chronic small vessel ischemic changes (Fazekas grade 1-2) are present and likely reflect comorbid cerebrovascular disease in the setting of hypertension and diabetes, but the degree of hippocampal atrophy exceeds what would be expected from vascular disease alone. No acute intracranial pathology. Clinical correlation recommended.

labs.interpretation: Abnormal values: Chloride 110 mEq/L (H), BUN 21 mg/dL (H), Glucose 122 mg/dL (H), HbA1c 6.3 % (H).

Neuropsychological_Battery.test: Rey Auditory Verbal Learning Test - Immediate Recall
Rey Auditory Verbal Learning Test - Delayed Recall

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: MMSE 21/30 points (H), MoCA 17/30 points (H), Rey Auditory Verbal Learning Test - Immediate Recall 3rd percentile percentile (H), Rey Auditory Verbal Learning Test - Delayed Recall 1st percentile percentile (H), Boston Naming Test 18th percentile percentile (H), Category Fluency (Animals) 8th percentile percentile (H), Letter Fluency (FAS) 22nd percentile percentile (H), Trail Making Test Part B 15th percentile percentile (H), Rey Complex Figure - Delayed Recall 5th percentile percentile (H).
abnormal_values_summary: Trail Making Test Part B: 15th percentile percentile (H)

findings.location: Diffuse cortical uptake involving prefrontal, precuneus, posterior cingulate, and lateral temporoparietal cortex

signal_characteristics.Distribution: Diffuse bilateral cortical binding with prominent uptake in posterior cingulate and precuneus, with relative sparing of primary sensorimotor and occipital cortex

additional_observations: Non-specific white matter retention within expected limits for age
No focal cortical asymmetry suggestive of atypical variant
output.impression: POSITIVE amyloid PET scan. The SUVr of 1.48 indicates moderate-to-high amyloid plaque density. Clinical correlation recommended.

findings.location: Bilateral temporoparietal cortex, posterior cingulate, and precuneus

signal_characteristics.FDG_PET: Significantly reduced FDG uptake in bilateral temporal and parietal association cortices with prominent posterior cingulate and precuneus involvement
signal_characteristics.Pattern: Bilateral temporoparietal and posterior cingulate hypometabolism with preserved metabolism in primary sensorimotor cortex, occipital cortex, basal ganglia, thalami, and cerebellum

output.impression: FDG-PET demonstrates the classic metabolic signature of Alzheimer disease with bilateral temporoparietal and posterior cingulate hypometabolism. Frontal involvement is mild, suggesting early-to-moderate stage. Clinical correlation recommended.

output.interpretation: Opening pressure: 15 cmH2O. WBC: 2 cells/uL (N/A PMN/N/A lymph). Protein: 45.6 mg/dL. Glucose: 72.6 mg/dL (ratio 0.58 (serum glucose 118 mg/dL)). Amyloid_beta_42: 362 pg/mL (reference >500 pg/mL) - DECREASED. Amyloid_beta_40: 7850 pg/mL (reference range 5000-12000 pg/mL) - Normal. Amyloid_beta_42_40_ratio: 0.046 (reference >0.065) - DECREASED. Phosphorylated_tau_181: 84 pg/mL (reference <60 pg/mL) - ELEVATED. Total_tau: 520 pg/mL (reference <400 pg/mL) - ELEVATED. 14-3-3_protein: Negative. RT-QuIC: Negative.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 7-8 Hz alpha activity over occipital regions, mildly slowed below the normal range of 8.5-12 Hz

artifacts.impact: Mild, does not significantly obscure underlying cerebral activity

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Not performed due to patient age
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Normal photic driving response, no photoparoxysmal response

output.impression: Mildly abnormal EEG. The posterior dominant rhythm is slowed at 7-8 Hz with intermittent diffuse theta slowing that is bitemporal predominant. No epileptiform discharges. No electrographic seizures. Clinical correlation recommended.
output.limitations: Hyperventilation was not performed. Study was performed in the waking state only; no sleep was captured.

interactions: Tamsulosin: no significant pharmacokinetic interaction, but both may lower blood pressure; monitor for orthostatic hypotension
No significant interactions with metformin, amlodipine, losartan, rosuvastatin, or aspirin
warnings: May cause bradycardia; baseline heart rate 76 bpm is reassuring, but periodic monitoring recommended
GI side effects (nausea, diarrhea, decreased appetite) common at initiation; patient already has mild weight loss, so monitor nutritional status
Avoid concurrent anticholinergic medications which would reduce efficacy
Use caution with beta-blockers if added in the future due to additive bradycardia risk
alternatives: Rivastigmine patch (4.6 mg/24h initially) - may have fewer GI side effects in patch form
Galantamine 4 mg twice daily - alternative cholinesterase inhibitor

differential.diagnosis: Vascular dementia or mixed Alzheimer/vascular dementia
differential.key_distinguishing: Patient has vascular risk factors (diabetes, hypertension) and MRI shows Fazekas 1-2 white matter changes, but the gradual progressive amnestic pattern, hippocampal atrophy disproportionate to vascular changes, and absence of stepwise decline or prominent executive dysfunction favor AD over pure vascular dementia
MMSE 21/30 is clearly below normal; functional decline confirmed by wife including loss of ability to manage finances and navigate; severity well beyond normal age-related forgetfulness
Insidious onset over two years; GDS 3/15 does not support significant depression; patient makes effort on testing; cognitive deficits are progressive and disproportionate to mood symptoms
No visual hallucinations, no parkinsonism or rigidity on exam, no REM sleep behavior disorder per wife, no fluctuating cognition or attention
No behavioral disinhibition, personality changes, or compulsive behaviors; memory impairment is the predominant symptom; social comportment is preserved

optimal_actions.action: Obtain detailed history from patient and informant, including timeline of cognitive changes, specific examples of functional decline, behavioral symptoms, and safety concerns
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Progressive amnestic cognitive decline over 2 years with clear functional impairment confirmed by spouse including loss of financial management, missed appointments, and navigational errors

optimal_actions.action: Perform comprehensive neurological examination with detailed mental status assessment including MMSE or MoCA
optimal_actions.expected_finding: MMSE 21/30 with prominent recall and orientation deficits; neurological exam otherwise unremarkable except for age-appropriate reduced vibration sense distally

optimal_actions.action: Order dementia screening labs: CBC, BMP, TSH, vitamin B12, folate, RPR, HIV, HbA1c
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Reversible causes excluded; mildly elevated glucose and HbA1c reflecting known diabetes

optimal_actions.action: Order brain MRI with volumetric protocol
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Bilateral hippocampal atrophy (Scheltens grade 2-3) with temporoparietal cortical volume loss; mild chronic small vessel disease
Multidomain amnestic profile with severely impaired delayed recall, reduced semantic fluency and naming, impaired executive function, consistent with AD

optimal_actions.action: Consider CSF biomarkers or amyloid PET for pathological confirmation of Alzheimer disease
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Decreased Abeta42, elevated phospho-tau and total tau confirming AD pathology (A+T+N+)

optimal_actions.action: Optimize vascular risk factor management including tighter glycemic control and blood pressure management
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Coordination with primary care for HbA1c target <7%, blood pressure optimization

optimal_actions.action: Initiate cholinesterase inhibitor therapy (donepezil 5 mg daily) after checking drug interactions
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No significant drug interactions with current medications

optimal_actions.action: Provide diagnosis disclosure and counseling to patient and family, including discussion of prognosis, safety planning including driving assessment, advance directives, and caregiver support resources
Schedule follow-up in 3-6 months to assess treatment response, monitor for side effects, and reassess cognition and function

critical_actions: Exclude reversible causes of dementia (labs: TSH, B12, RPR, HIV)
Perform standardized cognitive assessment (MMSE, MoCA, or neuropsychological battery)
Obtain collateral history from spouse or other informant
Assess functional status and safety including driving, cooking, and medication management
Address vascular risk factors (diabetes, hypertension) as modifiable contributors to cognitive decline
Discuss diagnosis, prognosis, and safety planning with patient and family
contraindicated_actions: Prescribing anticholinergic medications (e.g., oxybutynin for BPH, diphenhydramine) which would worsen cognitive function
Prescribing benzodiazepines without careful consideration of fall risk and cognitive side effects
Attributing symptoms to normal aging without formal cognitive evaluation
Starting memantine monotherapy in mild disease (not indicated until moderate-to-severe stage)
Ordering brain biopsy in the absence of atypical or rapidly progressive features
Prescribing antipsychotics without a clear behavioral indication given increased mortality risk in dementia patients
Discontinuing diabetes or hypertension medications, as vascular risk factor management is important for slowing cognitive decline
key_reasoning_points: Progressive episodic memory loss over 2 years with insidious onset is the hallmark of early Alzheimer disease
Clear functional decline (inability to manage finances, missed appointments, navigational errors, difficulty with assembly tasks) distinguishes dementia from mild cognitive impairment
MMSE 21/30 with complete failure of delayed recall (0/3 at 5 minutes even with cues) is characteristic of hippocampal-type amnesia seen in AD
Normal neurological examination aside from cognitive deficits argues against Lewy body dementia (no parkinsonism), vascular dementia (no focal deficits), and FTD (preserved social comportment)
Strong family history with mother and maternal aunt having dementia significantly increases pretest probability for AD
MRI showing bilateral hippocampal atrophy (Scheltens 2-3) with temporoparietal cortical volume loss is the classic structural imaging pattern of AD

Coexisting vascular risk factors (diabetes, hypertension) and mild white matter disease on MRI warrant consideration of mixed pathology, but the degree of hippocampal atrophy and the amnestic clinical phenotype point to AD as the primary driver
Normal dementia screening labs rule out treatable causes and support a primary neurodegenerative etiology

key_educational_points: Early AD typically presents with progressive episodic memory loss as the predominant symptom
Vascular risk factors such as diabetes and hypertension are modifiable contributors to cognitive decline and should be optimized
APOE e4 homozygosity confers the highest genetic risk for sporadic late-onset AD
CSF biomarkers (Abeta42, phospho-tau, total tau) provide in vivo confirmation of Alzheimer pathology
Cholinesterase inhibitors are first-line pharmacotherapy for mild-to-moderate AD
Mixed AD and vascular pathology is common and does not preclude an AD diagnosis when the amnestic phenotype predominates
